items.headline: Top biotech deals in February 2026
items.preview: February 2026 saw significant acquisitions and licensing deals in biotech.
items.article:
The month of February 2026 saw one of the biggest acquisitions in the biopharma space in recent times. California-based Arcellx’s stocks have exploded by 77% since Gilead declared that it would take over the company for $7.8 billion. Arcellx’s lead phase 2 candidate, a BCMA-directed CAR T-cell therapy for multiple myeloma named anito-cel, will soon be under the wing of the pharma giant.

Moreover, American giant Lilly has made plans to purchase Massachusetts-based Orna Therapeutics for up to $2.4 billion. GSK is taking over Quebec-based 35Pharma for $950 million, which includes HS235, an activin signalling inhibitor in the clinic to treat heart and lung diseases. Why this matters: These deals highlight the ongoing trend of consolidation and investment in innovative therapies within the biotech sector.

items.headline: Targeting Methionine Restriction in Cancer Therapy
items.preview: Methionine restriction shows promise as a cancer therapy by targeting metabolic vulnerabilities.
items.article:
Cancer treatment has long grappled with the challenge of selectively targeting tumor cells while sparing normal tissues. Methionine restriction (MR), an emerging dietary strategy, capitalizes on this by limiting the availability of methionine, impairing malignant cell growth. Recent advances have begun to unravel the intricate molecular underpinnings and clinical potential of MR.

Preclinical investigations have shown that MR disrupts essential biochemical pathways in cancer cells, leading to reduced proliferation rates and cell cycle arrest. Why this matters: MR may transform oncology by exploiting a fundamental metabolic dependency intrinsic to many cancers, offering a low-toxicity adjunct to existing therapies.

items.headline: Combination GLP-1 Therapy Reduces Fat Mass While Preserving Lean Muscle
items.preview: A clinical trial shows that combining GLP-1 therapy with bimagrumab effectively reduces fat mass.
items.article:
A groundbreaking clinical trial has revealed that combining the GLP-1 receptor agonist semaglutide with bimagrumab produces remarkable synergistic effects in treating obesity. This approach not only delivers pronounced weight loss but also preserves lean body mass, marking a pivotal advancement in obesity therapeutics.

The BELIEVE study demonstrated that the combination therapy resulted in an impressive average weight loss of 22.1%, with 92.8% of this loss from fat mass. Why this matters: This dual-action therapy may redefine standards of care in obesity management by emphasizing both fat mass reduction and muscle conservation.

items.headline: UCB licenses Antengene’s ATG-201 in $1.1B autoimmune deal
items.preview: UCB has secured a licensing deal for Antengene's ATG-201, a bispecific T-cell engager.
items.article:
UCB is adding a new immune-cell weapon to its immunology arsenal by striking a global licensing deal with Antengene for ATG-201, a CD19/CD3 bispecific T-cell engager designed to deplete B cells. Under the agreement, UCB gains worldwide exclusive rights to develop and commercialize ATG-201.

Antengene will receive $80 million upfront and can earn up to about $1.1 billion in additional milestones. Why this matters: This deal represents a significant advancement in autoimmune disease treatment, leveraging next-generation biologics for deeper, more durable effects.

items.headline: TASL protein required for age-related B cell emergence in lupus
items.preview: The adaptor protein TASL is crucial for B cell activation and lupus development in mice.
items.article:
The autoimmune disease systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is associated with genetic variants in the X-linked gene CXORF21, which encodes the protein TASL. This study investigates the role of TASL in the humoral immune response and lupus development in a murine model. It finds that TASL is required for full B cell activation via TLR9 stimulation and is crucial for the emergence of age-associated B cells.

Deletion of TASL prevents the onset of autoimmunity in the B6.MRLlpr model of lupus. Why this matters: Understanding the role of TASL could provide insights into therapeutic targets for treating lupus and other autoimmune diseases.
